summary.name: Tulsi (Holy Basil)
summary.scientific_name: Ocimum tenuiflorum L. (syn. Ocimum sanctum L.)

plant_profile.history: Unique History: Tulsi (literally meaning 'the incomparable one') is the most sacred plant in Hinduism and is worshipped as an incarnation of the Goddess Lakshmi. Beyond its spiritual significance, it has been used as a principal medicinal herb in Ayurveda for over 5,000 years. Described extensively in Charaka Samhita, Sushruta Samhita, and Bhavaprakasha Nighantu. Recognized in the Ayurvedic Pharmacopoeia of India (API) and endorsed by the Ministry of AYUSH as a critical herb for respiratory and immune health. Physical Characteristics: An erect, much-branched, aromatic subshrub or perennial herb, growing 30-100 cm tall. Leaves are simple, opposite, petiolate, ovate-oblong, with an entire or serrate margin, and are highly aromatic. Two main cultivated varieties exist: 'Rama Tulsi' (green-leaved, milder) and 'Krishna Tulsi' or 'Shyama Tulsi' (purple-leaved, considered more potent). Flowers are small, purplish, arranged in elongated racemes. Native to the Indian subcontinent and widely cultivated throughout Southeast Asia. It thrives in tropical and subtropical climates. Specific Chemical Compounds: Key bioactive compounds include essential oil (0.7-1.5% of fresh herb weight) predominantly containing eugenol (40-70%), methyl eugenol, β-caryophyllene, carvacrol, linalool, and 1,8-cineole. Non-volatile compounds include rosmarinic acid (the primary water-soluble phenolic), ursolic acid, oleanolic acid, chlorogenic acid, apigenin, luteolin, orientin, vicenin, and ocimumosides A and B. The proportion of these varies significantly between Rama (green) and Krishna (purple) varieties, with Krishna Tulsi generally having higher eugenol content. Strict Standardizations: As per API, the whole plant (Panchanga) and leaves of Ocimum tenuiflorum (synonym: O. sanctum) are standardized. Quality parameters include total ash, acid-insoluble ash, alcohol-soluble extractive, water-soluble extractive, volatile oil content (not less than 0.4%), and foreign matter limits. HPTLC/GC-MS fingerprinting of the essential oil profile (with eugenol as a primary marker) is used for quality assurance and species authentication, aligning with WHO quality control guidelines for herbal materials.

ayurvedic_properties.dosha_modulation: Primarily pacifies Vata and Kapha doshas due to its Ushna Virya (hot potency) and Tikshna (sharp) Guna. It may slightly increase Pitta in excess due to its heating nature. It is considered a Sattvic herb that purifies the mind and spirit.

medicinal_uses.respiratory_health: Tulsi is arguably the most important Ayurvedic herb for the respiratory system. It acts as a powerful expectorant (Kaphaghna), antimicrobial, and bronchodilator. It is a primary remedy for common cold (Pratishyaya), cough (Kasa), bronchitis, asthma (Shwasa), and influenza. WHO monographs and AYUSH publications strongly support its traditional use in respiratory ailments.
medicinal_uses.adaptogenic_and_anti_stress: Modern pharmacology recognizes Tulsi as a premier adaptogen. Its active compounds, including ocimumosides A and B, help the body adapt to various physical, chemical, metabolic, and psychological stressors, normalizing cortisol pathways and supporting adrenal function.
medicinal_uses.immunomodulatory: Enhances innate and adaptive immune responses. It boosts the production of T-helper cells, NK cells, and interferon, making it valuable for preventing recurrent infections and supporting immune surveillance.
medicinal_uses.antimicrobial_broad_spectrum: Possesses potent antibacterial, antiviral, antifungal, and anti-malarial activity. Eugenol and other volatile compounds in its essential oil are effective against a wide range of pathogens, including antibiotic-resistant strains like MRSA.
medicinal_uses.cardioprotective_and_metabolic_regulatio: Exhibits lipid-lowering, blood-pressure-regulating, and blood-sugar-modulating properties. It supports cardiovascular health and is being studied for its potential in managing metabolic syndrome.
medicinal_uses.chemical_constituents: Major bioactive compounds include eugenol (the primary essential oil component, responsible for its distinctive clove-like aroma and antimicrobial activity), rosmarinic acid (a potent antioxidant and anti-inflammatory), ursolic acid (anti-inflammatory, anti-cancer), oleanolic acid, carvacrol, linalool, β-caryophyllene, ocimumosides A and B (adaptogenic), apigenin, luteolin, and orientin (flavonoids).

preparation_methods.fresh_leaf_juice__svarasa_: Crush 10-20 fresh Tulsi leaves and extract the juice. This is considered the most potent form for respiratory infections, cough, and fever. Mix with equal quantity of honey and a pinch of black pepper (Maricha) for enhanced expectorant action.
preparation_methods.tulsi_tea_infusion__phanta_: Steep 5-10 fresh or dried Tulsi leaves in a cup of boiling water for 5-10 minutes. Add honey and lemon juice. This is a daily health-promoting beverage for immune support and stress relief.
preparation_methods.decoction__kwatha_: Boil 10-15g of dried Tulsi leaves/whole herb in 200ml water until reduced to 50ml. Used for acute fevers, severe cough, and respiratory tract infections.
preparation_methods.powder__churna_: Shade-dried leaves or whole herb are finely powdered. Consumed with warm water or honey. Less potent than fresh preparations but convenient for daily use. Used in various classical formulations.
preparation_methods.essential_oil: Tulsi essential oil (extracted by steam distillation of the leaves) is used in aromatherapy for respiratory ailments and stress. It can be diluted in a carrier oil for topical application over the chest and sinuses.

dosage.adults: Fresh Leaf Juice (Svarasa): 10-20 ml, 2-3 times daily. Dried Leaf Powder (Churna): 1-3 grams per day with warm water or honey. Decoction: 50-100 ml per day in divided doses. Fresh leaves: 5-10 leaves chewed daily for general wellness.
dosage.children: Fresh leaves: 2-5 leaves chewed or as a mild tea, 1-2 times daily for colds and cough. Juice: 2-5 ml with honey. Generally very safe for pediatric use in dietary/culinary amounts. Therapeutic doses should be under medical supervision.
dosage.elderly: Standard adult dosages are generally safe and highly recommended for immune support, respiratory health, and cardioprotection. Monitor blood sugar and blood pressure if on concurrent allopathic medications.

contraindications: Due to its Ushna Virya (heating potency) and blood-thinning properties (eugenol is a known anti-platelet agent), it should be used with caution in individuals with severe Pitta aggravation, active bleeding disorders, or those undergoing anticoagulant therapy. Some traditional texts advise moderate rather than excessive consumption during pregnancy due to its heating nature and potential mild emmenagogue effect, though culinary amounts are considered safe.
side_effects: Generally very well-tolerated and considered one of the safest Ayurvedic herbs. Rare adverse effects may include mild gastrointestinal discomfort at very high doses, or a temporary increase in Pitta symptoms (acidity, heat) in sensitive individuals. Eugenol in the essential oil can be hepatotoxic at very high, non-therapeutic concentrations.
drug_interactions: May potentiate the effects of anticoagulant/antiplatelet drugs (Warfarin, Aspirin, Clopidogrel) due to the anti-platelet activity of eugenol. May interact with antidiabetic drugs by enhancing their hypoglycemic effects. May theoretically interact with anti-hypertensive drugs. It is advisable to discontinue therapeutic doses of Tulsi at least 2 weeks before any planned surgical procedure due to its blood-thinning potential.
safety.toxicity_notes: GRAS Status: Tulsi has an exceptionally well-documented history of safe traditional use as both a food/tea and a medicine. It is listed in the API and extensively recognized by the Ministry of AYUSH. WHO acknowledges its traditional safety profile. No significant adverse effects have been reported at recommended therapeutic doses in clinical studies or traditional practice.
